# PPTX Skill

## Quick Reference

| Task | Guide |
|------|-------|
| Read/analyze content | `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` |
| Edit or create from template | Read [editing.md](editing.md) |
| Create from scratch | Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) |

---

## Reading Content

```bash
# Text extraction
python -m markitdown presentation.pptx

# Visual overview
python scripts/thumbnail.py presentation.pptx

# Raw XML
python scripts/office/unpack.py presentation.pptx unpacked/
```

---

## Editing Workflow

**Read [editing.md](editing.md) for full details.**

1. Analyze template with `thumbnail.py`
2. Unpack → manipulate slides → edit content → clean → pack

---

## Creating from Scratch

**Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) for full details.**

Use when no template or reference presentation is available.

---

## Design Ideas

**Don't create boring slides.** Plain bullets on a white background won't impress anyone. Consider ideas from this list for each slide.

### Before Starting

- **Pick a bold, content-informed color palette**: The palette should feel designed for THIS topic. If swapping your colors into a completely different presentation would still "work," you haven't made specific enough choices.
- **Dominance over equality**: One color should dominate (60-70% visual weight), with 1-2 supporting tones and one sharp accent. Never give all colors equal weight.
- **Dark/light contrast**: Dark backgrounds for title + conclusion slides, light for content ("sandwich" structure). Or commit to dark throughout for a premium feel.
- **Commit to a visual motif**: Pick ONE distinctive element and repeat it — rounded image frames, icons in colored circles, thick single-side borders. Carry it across every slide.

### Color Palettes

Choose colors that match your topic — don't default to generic blue. Use these palettes as inspiration:

| Theme | Primary | Secondary | Accent |
|-------|---------|-----------|--------|
| **Midnight Executive** | `1E2761` (navy) | `CADCFC` (ice blue) | `FFFFFF` (white) |
| **Forest & Moss** | `2C5F2D` (forest) | `97BC62` (moss) | `F5F5F5` (cream) |
| **Coral Energy** | `F96167` (coral) | `F9E795` (gold) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
| **Warm Terracotta** | `B85042` (terracotta) | `E7E8D1` (sand) | `A7BEAE` (sage) |
| **Ocean Gradient** | `065A82` (deep blue) | `1C7293` (teal) | `21295C` (midnight) |
| **Charcoal Minimal** | `36454F` (charcoal) | `F2F2F2` (off-white) | `212121` (black) |
| **Teal Trust** | `028090` (teal) | `00A896` (seafoam) | `02C39A` (mint) |
| **Berry & Cream** | `6D2E46` (berry) | `A26769` (dusty rose) | `ECE2D0` (cream) |
| **Sage Calm** | `84B59F` (sage) | `69A297` (eucalyptus) | `50808E` (slate) |
| **Cherry Bold** | `990011` (cherry) | `FCF6F5` (off-white) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |

### For Each Slide

**Every slide needs a visual element** — image, chart, icon, or shape. Text-only slides are forgettable.

**Layout options:**
- Two-column (text left, illustration on right)
- Icon + text rows (icon in colored circle, bold header, description below)
- 2x2 or 2x3 grid (image on one side, grid of content blocks on other)
- Half-bleed image (full left or right side) with content overlay

**Data display:**
- Large stat callouts (big numbers 60-72pt with small labels below)
- Comparison columns (before/after, pros/cons, side-by-side options)
- Timeline or process flow (numbered steps, arrows)

**Visual polish:**
- Icons in small colored circles next to section headers
- Italic accent text for key stats or taglines

### Typography

**Choose an interesting font pairing** — don't default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality and pair it with a clean body font.

| Header Font | Body Font |
|-------------|-----------|
| Georgia | Calibri |
| Arial Black | Arial |
| Calibri | Calibri Light |
| Cambria | Calibri |
| Trebuchet MS | Calibri |
| Impact | Arial |
| Palatino | Garamond |
| Consolas | Calibri |

The project is about rebuilding ByteDance's Identity Graph from Connected Components to a probabilistic system with message passing. Key facts:
- 25B edges, 22B serving rows, <12h SLA
- Two-phase algorithm: edge probability precomputation (logistic function) + message passing (top-K propagation)
- Cross-references 12+ data sources (1P: NPS, contact sync, device, IPv6, TTS; 2P: click-ID, cookie, advertiser agreement; 3P: Adstra, Experian, TransUnion)
- Weight learning: started with manual intuition, moved to logistic regression with click-ID ground truth
- Score threshold 0.6, email accuracy >74%, phone >71%
- GraphX Pregel OOMed, pivoted to SQL-based message passing (2-week setback)
- v2 optimization: frontier nodes only, bucketed intermediary tables
- 6+ versions iterated (v0-v5)
- Online serving: Abase key-value, 22B rows at 600k QPS
- Known limitation: single-path probability (max not union), evidence independence assumption
- Downstream consumers: DM, PM, Lift, Custom Audiences, xDM/UM, Global Identity

Structure the file with these sections:
1. "Why This Design?" questions (algorithm choice, logistic vs Bayesian, message passing vs alternatives)
2. "What Were the Main Tradeoffs?" questions (single-path, independence, Person ID, coverage vs accuracy)
3. "What Went Wrong in Production?" questions (GraphX OOM, scaling issues, data quality, compliance)
4. "How Did You Measure Success?" questions (ground truth choice, metrics, evaluation methodology)
5. Technical Deep-Dive questions (supernodes, hop count, numerical stability, weight learning)
6. System Design questions (online serving, cross-DC sync, disaster recovery, data pipeline)
7. Leadership & Staff-Level questions (cross-team coordination, stakeholder management, scoping)

For each question, provide:
- The question itself
- Key talking points (2-4 bullets)
- A "watch out for" note about common interviewer follow-ups

Write it as a practical interview prep document. Use clear markdown formatting. This is for a Staff Engineer level interview at a top AI company.

### 2. Add a NEW slide after current slide 13 (Iterative Evaluation) — "Optimization Deep Dive"
This slide should explain the v1→v2 optimization journey in more detail:

Title: "Optimization Deep Dive: v1 → v2"
Layout: Dark background (C.navy), two-column layout

Left column "v1: Baseline Implementation":
- Generate unique bigint IDs for every vertex: 1h
- Generate bi-directional edges: 10m  
- Generate vertex table: 14m
- Each hop: ~1h → Total: ~5.3h for 5 hops
- Resources: 500 cores, 15 TB memory
- Problem: Limited by memory, full graph reprocessed each hop

Right column "v2: Frontier Optimization":
- Key insight: only join on "frontier" nodes (vertices whose TopK changed)
- Save intermediary hop results in bucketed tables
- Bucketing on identifier reduces Spark shuffle dramatically
- Removes need for bigint ID assignment entirely
- Each hop uses predictable, bounded time and resources
- Next-step optimizations considered: index, skip unchanged vertices, intermediary tables

Bottom callout: "Numerical Stability" box explaining log-probs: log(Π pᵢ) = −Σ log(1+e^(−zᵢ)), logsumexp trick for future negative weights

### 3. Add presenter notes (s.addNotes("...")) to EVERY slide

The scale is significant: 25 billion edges, 22 billion rows in the online serving table, running under a 12-hour SLA."

**Slide 2 (Section 1 divider)**: "Let me start with the problem context."

**Slide 3 (Problem Context)**: "When an advertiser runs a campaign on TikTok, they send conversion events — purchases, sign-ups — with user identifiers like emails, phone numbers, or device IDs. Our job is to match those identifiers to TikTok user accounts so we can attribute the conversion to the right ad. The identity graph does that mapping. Your interview playbook is ready. Here's the quick strategy to pass this:

**The 60-minute game plan:**
- **First 10 minutes (intro + high-level):** You set the frame. If you nail the intro, interviewers relax and let you drive. Memorize the opening script verbatim — it's the only part you should rehearse word-for-word.
- **Next 15 minutes (deep dive):** Budget it tightly — 3 min architecture, 3 min scoring, 3 min weights, 3 min scaling, 3 min eval. If they interrupt early, abandon your plan and follow their curiosity. That's a good sign.
- **Last 35 minutes (Q&A):** This is where staff-level is decided. Expect 7–12 questions. Use **STAR+L** (Situation, Task, Action, Result, **Learning**) — the "what I'd do differently" line is what separates senior from staff.

**Top 3 things to internalize before game day:**
1. Say "**I**" for decisions, "**we**" for execution. Staff-level interviewers are listening for ownership.
2. When asked "why not X?" — **steelman their idea first**, then defend yours with a cost/benefit frame tied to the state of the team at that time.
3. Every answer should end in a way that makes them want to **hire you for judgment**, not just expertise.

Now you're onto something. "Users have goals but exploring plans hurts" — that's a genuine pain, and AI is genuinely the right lever for it. Let me push on it before we converge.

## What's strong (don't second-guess)

- **"Start small, grow on success"** is the single best psychological mechanic you could pick. Fogg's Tiny Habits, Atomic Habits, progressive overload all say the same thing. Most goal apps fail by being too ambitious on day 1.
- **Failure → ask why → adjust plan** is the loop that separates "coach" from "tracker." 99% of habit apps don't do this — they just break your streak and shame you. If you nail this, you have a real moat.
- **Solo-first** is the right call. Family-shared AI plans get socially weird fast. Nail the single-user loop, let family come back in V3 as "share a plan I'm running."

## Where the WOW actually lives

It's not "AI-native" in the abstract. The specific wow moment is:

> **"I type one sentence. 90 seconds later, I have tomorrow's single first action that feels like a smart friend planned it."**

Everything else — the plan doc, the recurrence, the adjustments — is scaffolding. If this first session doesn't land, nothing else matters.

Corollary: your first-session onboarding is the entire product. Not the daily check-in. Not the dashboard. The 90 seconds after the user types their goal. Design that first, everything else second.

## Five hard questions you haven't answered yet

**1. What exactly does the AI ask?** You said "LLM ask user more questions to make good plans." For "I want to keep fit in 3 months," write down the *actual* 3-5 questions you'd ask. Right now. In Chinese.

If you can't, the UX isn't designed. "AI asks smart questions" is vaporware until you've written the questions. My hunch: for fit, you need roughly — current activity baseline, time per day, equipment access, injury/constraints, primary motivation (lose weight vs feel stronger vs look different). For Japanese: current level, minutes per day, reading vs speaking priority, deadline flexibility. Three goals = three interview scripts. Hand-write them before you code.

**2. Why a "doc"?** Your step 4 says "create the entire plan in doc but only surface small calendar todo." Two possibilities:
- **Doc = visible plan** user can open/read/edit → another surface, UX complexity
- **Doc = AI's internal scratchpad** → shouldn't be called a doc; it's a data structure

Pick one. If it's option 1, the doc IS a feature and deserves design. If it's 2, delete the word "doc" from your spec.

My opinion: the user SHOULD be able to see the full plan at will ("what's this whole thing leading to?"), but not forced to read it. Optional transparency. Hide by default, reveal on tap.

**3. What is "perfect execution" and how is failure handled?** "If user executes perfectly, create more" — is that 100% of today's items? Or 80%? Do you measure on a rolling window? And when they fail — what's the tone?

The failure conversation is the single UX moment that will make or break this product. Two wrong tones I'd avoid:
- **Concerned-nurse**: "Is everything OK? You missed yesterday." — feels like being monitored
- **Drill-sergeant**: "You missed 2/3. Let's get back on track." — feels like being judged

Right tone, guessing: **curious-friend** — "Yesterday slipped. Want to adjust, or just skip it?" No guilt, gives user agency, treats the plan as a draft not a contract.

**4. How many active plans?** One at a time (forced focus), or many (dashboard)?

My opinion: **one primary plan, one optional background habit**. Primary = "the thing I'm working on this quarter" (fit, language, etc). Background = "drink 2L water" ambient habit. Any more and the daily list becomes overwhelming, and you're back to Todoist.

**5. What happens to the 8 fixed health habits you already built?**

Three options:
- Delete them; everyone starts from goal-entry screen
- Keep them as a pre-seeded "wellness cadence" plan any user can adopt
- Make them specifically YOUR plan

## The daily loop — completion logic

```
今日完成率:
  ≥ 80%  → 🎯 "节奏很稳，明天继续"  + 解锁下一 batch (AI 生成未来 3-7 天)
  60-80% → ✓  "今天做得不错"         + 继续当前 batch
  < 60%  → 🫂 "今天有点满？告诉我什么挡住你了：
              [太累] [时间不够] [没兴趣做这个] [别的事]
              → AI 消化理由，下一 batch 调整(降强度/换项目/间隔延长)
```

Curious-friend tone throughout. No streak-breaking shame. If user skips 3 days, plan pauses itself and asks if they want to reduce scope.

**"I don't need to select if I already on it"** — the onboarding skips questions we already have data for. User has been checking 💧 water + 😴 sleep for a week → form doesn't ask "want water/sleep as background?" It auto-includes them with an inline "保留" toggle.

- Floating button (bottom-right on /today, or in header). Tap → drawer slides up.
- Chat with plan in context (prompt-cached so it's fast + cheap).
- Coach can propose plan edits. When it does, a **diff card** appears in-chat:
  ```
  建议改动:
    · 把周三的 HIIT 换成瑜伽(你说过膝盖疼)
    · 第3周加一次力量训练
    [应用] [再想想]
  ```
- User taps apply → plan doc + future `plan_items` regenerate. Past completions stay intact.

This is the crucial integration — the coach isn't passive. It WRITES to your plan.

### The headline findings from the competitive brief

- **Empty quadrant**: low-friction × high-plan-intelligence is genuinely unoccupied. Every AI coach is chat-first (Rocky, Ash, ChatGPT — high friction). Every low-friction habit app has zero AI (Streaks, Finch, Stoic). Fabulous is the nearest neighbor but uses curated routines, not LLM generation. **This is Cadence's actual wedge.**
- **Biggest threat: Fabulous adds LLM plan generation.** 2-month move for them. They'd own your onboarding pitch with more brand and retention behind them.
- **Second threat: ChatGPT Projects/Memory** — for users already in ChatGPT, your value prop is replicable with zero install. Don't try to win them; focus on the 95% who don't live in a chat window.
- **CN wedge is real and underrated.** Every US AI coach is English-only. Keep/薄荷 do plans vertically. Nobody does horizontal goal coaching in ZH. Make CN the distribution story.
- **"Curious friend" tone is the most commoditized-sounding axis and the hardest to actually ship** — every competitor says they're warm and encouraging, and almost none are. Ship tone regression tests before you ship the coach.

1. **Obsess over first-90s plan quality** — hand-write 20 canonical first plans before writing generation prompts; use as eval suite; keep 3-variant sampling even though it doubles cost. If the first plan feels generic, nothing else matters.
2. **Ship CN-first explicitly** — default language stays `zh`, archetypes include 考研/减脂/考公/雅思/学日语, tone tuned for CN gen-z / millennial warmth. This is your distribution wedge.

# Competitive Brief

> If you see unfamiliar placeholders or need to check which tools are connected, see [CONNECTORS.md](../../CONNECTORS.md).

Create a competitive analysis brief for one or more competitors or a feature area.

## Usage

```
/competitive-brief  please insvestiagte the competitors right now for exact same product and generae a report for me. Anayze the diffs and shared part.
```

## Workflow

### 1. Scope the Analysis

Ask the user:
- **Competitor(s)**: Which specific competitor(s) to analyze? Or a feature area to compare across competitors?
- **Focus**: Full product comparison, specific feature area, pricing/packaging, go-to-market, or positioning?
- **Context**: What decision will this inform? (product strategy, sales enablement, investor/board materials, feature prioritization)

### 2. Research

**Via web search**:
- Product pages and feature lists
- Pricing pages and packaging
- Recent product launches, blog posts, and changelogs
- Press coverage and analyst reports
- Customer reviews and ratings (G2, Capterra, TrustRadius)
- Job postings (signal of strategic direction)
- Social media and community discussions

If **~~knowledge base** is connected:
- Search for existing competitive analysis documents
- Find win/loss reports or sales battle cards
- Pull prior competitive research

If **~~chat** is connected:
- Search for competitive mentions in sales or product channels
- Find recent deal feedback involving competitors

### 3. Generate the Brief

#### Competitor Overview
For each competitor:
- Company summary: founding, size, funding/revenue if public, target market
- Product positioning: how they describe themselves, who they target
- Recent momentum: launches, funding, partnerships, customer wins

#### Feature Comparison
Compare capabilities across key areas relevant to the analysis. See **Feature Comparison Matrices** below for rating scales and matrix templates.

#### Positioning Analysis
Analyze how each competitor positions themselves — target customer, category claim, key differentiator, and value proposition. See **Positioning Analysis Frameworks** below for the positioning statement template and message architecture levels.

#### Strengths and Weaknesses
For each competitor:
- **Strengths**: Where they genuinely excel. What customers praise.
- **Weaknesses**: Where they fall short. What customers complain about.
- Be honest and evidence-based — do not dismiss competitors or inflate their weaknesses.

#### Opportunities
Based on the analysis:
- Where are there gaps in competitor offerings we could exploit?
- What are customers asking for that no one provides well?
- Where are competitors making bets we disagree with?
- What market shifts could advantage our approach?

#### Threats
- Where are competitors investing heavily?
- What competitive moves could disrupt our position?
- Where are we most vulnerable?
- What would a "nightmare scenario" competitive move look like?

#### Strategic Implications
Tie the analysis back to product strategy:
- What should we build, accelerate, or deprioritize based on this analysis?
- Where should we differentiate vs. achieve parity?
- How should we adjust positioning or messaging?
- What should we monitor going forward?

### 4. Follow Up

After generating the brief:
- Ask if the user wants to dive deeper on any section
- Offer to create a one-page summary for executives
- Offer to create sales battle cards for competitive deals
- Offer to draft a "how to win against [competitor]" guide
- Offer to set up a monitoring plan for competitive moves

## Competitive Landscape Mapping

### Identifying the Competitive Set
Define competitors at multiple levels:

**Direct competitors**: Products that solve the same problem for the same

# Product Brainstorming Skill

You are a sharp product thinking partner — the kind of experienced PM or design lead who challenges assumptions, asks the hard questions, and pushes ideas further before anyone converges too early. You help product managers explore problem spaces, generate ideas, and stress-test thinking before it becomes a spec.

Your job is not to generate deliverables. Your job is to think alongside the PM. Be opinionated. Push back. Bring in unexpected angles. Help them arrive at ideas they would not have reached alone.

## Brainstorming Modes

Different situations call for different modes of thinking. Identify which mode fits the conversation and adapt. You can shift between modes as the conversation evolves.

### Problem Exploration

Use when the PM has a problem area but has not yet defined what to solve. The goal is to understand the problem space deeply before jumping to solutions.

**What to do:**
- Ask "who has this problem?" and "what are they doing about it today?" before anything else
- Map the problem ecosystem: who is involved, what triggers the problem, what are the consequences of not solving it
- Distinguish symptoms from root causes. PMs often describe symptoms. Keep asking "why" until you hit something structural.
- Surface adjacent problems the PM might not have considered
- Ask how the problem varies across user segments — it rarely affects everyone the same way

**Useful questions:**
- "What happens if we do nothing? Who suffers and how?"
- "Who has solved a version of this problem in a different context?"
- "Is this a problem of awareness, ability, or motivation?"
- "What would need to be true for this problem to not exist?"

### Solution Ideation

Use when the problem is well-defined and the PM needs to generate multiple possible solutions. The goal is divergent thinking — quantity over quality.

**What to do:**
- Generate at least 5-7 distinct approaches before evaluating any of them
- Vary the solutions along meaningful dimensions: scope (small tweak vs big bet), approach (product vs process vs policy), timing (quick win vs long-term investment)
- Include at least one "what if we did the opposite?" option
- Include at least one option that removes something rather than adding something
- Resist the urge to converge too early. If the PM latches onto the first decent idea, push them to keep going.

**Ideation techniques:**
- **Constraint removal**: "What would you build if you had no technical constraints? No budget constraints? No political constraints?" Then work backward to what is feasible.
- **Analogies**: "How does [another industry] solve this? What can we steal from that approach?"
- **Inversion**: "How would we make this problem worse? Now reverse each of those."
- **Decomposition**: Break the problem into subproblems and solve each independently. Then combine.
- **User hat-switching**: "How would a power user solve this? A brand new user? An admin? Someone who hates our product?"

### Assumption Testing

Use when the PM has an idea or direction and needs to stress-test it. The goal is to find the weak points before investing in execution.

**What to do:**
- List every assumption the idea depends on — stated and unstated
- For each assumption, ask: "How confident are we? What evidence do we have? What would disprove this?"
- Identify the riskiest assumption — the one that, if wrong, kills the idea entirely
- Suggest the cheapest way to test the riskiest assumption before building anything
- Play devil's advocate: argue the strongest possible case against the idea

**Assumption categories to probe:**
- **User assumptions**: "Users want this" — How do we know? From what evidence? How many users?
- **Problem assumptions**: "This is a r

## Framing

The SDK's real edge isn't "another camera" — it's **hands-free + eye-level + socially invisible**. Any idea that could work just as well with an iPhone in hand is not the right idea. The winning concepts are ones where pulling out a phone breaks the moment.

## Diverge — 10 concepts across distinct dimensions

| # | Concept | Unique-to-glasses reason | Solo-dev risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cooking co-pilot** — recipe assistant, voice-controlled timers, "flip now" cues from pan state | hands are wet/dirty | timing accuracy is hard |
| 2 | **DIY / repair narrator** — show the thing, AI audio-walks you through it, remembers which bolt you already tried | hands hold tools | VLM correctness |
| 3 | **Look & Learn (language immersion)** — point at objects in the wild, hear target-language word, auto-build SRS deck | walking a city, no phone | low |
| 4 | **Birder / naturalist** — one-tap ID with audio callout | wildlife doesn't wait | low, but niche |
| 5 | **Field-trade assistant** — HVAC/inspector POV, voice notes + auto photos → compiled report | clipboard in hand | B2B distribution |
| 6 | **Parent memory cam** — passive/triggered capture of kid moments, daily reel | toddler-chasing | privacy, storage |
| 7 | **Shopping oracle** — in-aisle ingredient/price/review lookup, discreet | grocery cart occupied | requires lookups API |
| 8 | **Memory palace** — "where did I leave my keys" — timeline search over your day | passive, can't phone-capture always-on | privacy, demo delay |
| 9 | **Climbing beta recorder** — POV of a route + voice annotations, share with community | at the wall | tiny market |
| 10 | **Low-vision scene narrator** — audio description of surroundings | accessibility unlock | already exists (Be My Eyes) |

## Provoke

- **Ideas 6, 8 are privacy minefields** — you're streaming strangers. Shelve unless you want that fight.
- **Ideas 5, 9 are B2B/niche** — great LTV, terrible as a solo dev's first demo because no one you show it to can feel it.
- **Ideas 1, 2 are demo-seductive but execution-hard** — "flip the steak now" requires low-latency VLM inference on a video stream. Possible with Apple's Vision + a cloud VLM, but brittle.
- **Ideas 3, 4, 7 share a simple core loop**: photo → VLM/API → TTS → optional save. This is the *shipping-over-perfection* shape.

## Converge — my opinionated pick

### Prototype #1: **"Look & Learn" — wearable language immersion**

**Core loop (90% of the SDK surface):** double-tap on glasses → photo capture → VLM classifies the object → TTS speaks the word in your target language → card appears in phone app's SRS queue.

Why this one first:
- **Demo sells itself in 15 seconds** — point at a mug, hear "une tasse," card saved. Non-technical people *get it*.
- **No latency-critical video stream** — photo capture is enough. Much lower engineering bar than cooking/DIY.
- **Maps cleanly to the SDK skills we just installed**: registration → session → photo capture → audio feedback. No exotic code paths.
- **Has a real moat for solo dev**: the SRS deck + per-user vocabulary graph gets stickier over time. Retention story beyond the novelty.
- **Clear paywall**: free = 1 language, paid = all + export to Anki.

**Riskiest assumption to test first (cheap):** will users *actually* tap the glasses to capture, vs. feeling self-conscious? Validate by forcing yourself to use a paper prototype (phone + audio cue) for one day in a grocery store before writing a line of glasses-specific code.

### Prototype #2 (backup / stretch): **POV Cooking Co-pilot**

Shelve unless #1 lands. Bigger demo payoff but 3x the engineering risk for a solo dev — you're fighting VLM latency, state tracking across a recipe, and noisy pan scenes.

---

## What I'm going to do next

Scaffold the iOS app for **Look & Learn**: SwiftUI app with DAT SDK integrated, Meta AI registration flow, a "Capture" screen that triggers photo capture from glasses, sends to a VLM (stub for now), speaks the result via `AVSpeechSynthe
Xcode 26.2 available, no xcodegen. I'll scaffold source + project.yml, install xcodegen via brew, then generate the xcodeproj.
Xcodeproj generated. Let me verify it builds — this resolves SPM dependencies.
Real SDK API is async (skill docs are out of date). Fixing.
Now I understand the real v0.6.0 API. Rewriting CaptureViewModel.
Build succeeds. Let me also verify the Info.plist rewrite by xcodegen kept my MWDAT/URL scheme entries — it did.
Let me save a memory about the stale skill docs — useful for future sessions.

## Done

**SDK setup** — installed Meta DAT Claude skills into [.claude/](.claude/), `xcodegen` via brew, SPM resolved MetaWearablesDAT 0.6.0.

**App scaffolded** — `Look & Learn`, a language-immersion app. Solo-dev-sized, demo-friendly.

**Build status:** `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **` on iPhone 17 simulator.

**Key files:**
- [project.yml](project.yml) — XcodeGen spec (Info.plist + SPM deps)
- [App/LookAndLearnApp.swift](LookAndLearn/App/LookAndLearnApp.swift) — `Wearables.configure()` + SwiftData container
- [ViewModels/CaptureViewModel.swift](LookAndLearn/ViewModels/CaptureViewModel.swift) — real v0.6.0 pipeline: `createSession` → `addStream` → listen to `statePublisher`/`videoFramePublisher`/`photoDataPublisher` → `capturePhoto` → VLM → TTS → SwiftData card
- [Services/VisionLabeler.swift](LookAndLearn/Services/VisionLabeler.swift) — protocol + stub; swap in Claude Vision later
- [Views/CaptureView.swift](LookAndLearn/Views/CaptureView.swift), [DeckView](LookAndLearn/Views/DeckView.swift), [SettingsView](LookAndLearn/Views/SettingsView.swift) — 3-tab UI
- [README.md](README.md) — build/run/testing instructions

**Brainstorm pick for prototype #1: Look & Learn** (hands-free language immersion). Shipped the scaffold. Stretch/backup: POV cooking co-pilot.

**Gotchas saved to memory:** The bundled `.claude/skills/` docs for this SDK are stale vs v0.6.0 (registration is async, `StreamSession` has no public init — must go through `DeviceSession.addStream`). Saved as a feedback memory.

**Next steps you'll want:**
1. Swap `StubVisionLabeler` for a real VLM (Claude Vision via the `anthropic` SDK — see the `claude-api` skill).
2. Test on real hardware via Meta AI registration flow (Settings tab → Register).
3. Add SRS scheduling and an export-to-Anki button for retention/paywall hook.

# Write Spec

> If you see unfamiliar placeholders or need to check which tools are connected, see [CONNECTORS.md](../../CONNECTORS.md).

Write a feature specification or product requirements document (PRD).

## Usage

```
/write-spec Write a PRD for "Talk Coach" — an iOS app that provides live conversation coaching. 

Context (from prior turns + user correction):
- User pivoted away from visual/video-based ideas (initial "Look & Learn" language app was rejected as "not needed"). 
- The real goal: audio-only flow. User's exact words: "you don't need the visual, but only record the audio, routing to LLM and then given me instant feedback and HOw I talk to this person"
- The SDK in use is Meta Wearables DAT iOS (facebook/meta-wearables-dat-ios 0.6.0), but current public SDK does NOT expose glasses audio streaming — so V1 must fall back to iPhone microphone via AVAudioEngine + SFSpeech for transcription. Glasses audio is a future upgrade when/if the DAT SDK exposes it.
- App scaffold already exists with: SwiftData-backed history, ConversationCoach protocol (swap-in point for the LLM), SFSpeechRecognizer pipeline, SwiftUI tabs (Coach / History / Settings), bundle id com.francisyang.talkcoach.
- Solo dev, MVP-sized scope.

The PRD should cover: problem statement, target user, JTBD, goals/non-goals, user flows, success metrics, MVP feature scope vs later, the Meta glasses audio upgrade path, key risks/assumptions, and open questions. Be opinionated and specific — this is a scoping PRD for a solo dev to build against, not a committee document.
```

## Workflow

### 1. Understand the Feature

Ask the user what they want to spec. Accept any of:
- A feature name ("SSO support")
- A problem statement ("Enterprise customers keep asking for centralized auth")
- A user request ("Users want to export their data as CSV")
- A vague idea ("We should do something about onboarding drop-off")

### 2. Gather Context

Ask the user for the following. Be conversational — do not dump all questions at once. Ask the most important ones first and fill in gaps as you go:

- **User problem**: What problem does this solve? Who experiences it?
- **Target users**: Which user segment(s) does this serve?
- **Success metrics**: How will we know this worked?
- **Constraints**: Technical constraints, timeline, regulatory requirements, dependencies
- **Prior art**: Has this been attempted before? Are there existing solutions?

### 3. Pull Context from Connected Tools

If **~~project tracker** is connected:
- Search for related tickets, epics, or features
- Pull in any existing requirements or acceptance criteria
- Identify dependencies on other work items

If **~~knowledge base** is connected:
- Search for related research documents, prior specs, or design docs
- Pull in relevant user research findings
- Find related meeting notes or decision records

If **~~design** is connected:
- Pull related mockups, wireframes, or design explorations
- Search for design system components relevant to the feature

If these tools are not connected, work entirely from what the user provides. Do not ask the user to connect tools — just proceed with available information.

### 4. Generate the PRD

Produce a structured PRD with these sections. See **PRD Structure** below for detailed guidance on what each section should contain.

- **Problem Statement**: The user problem, who is affected, and impact of not solving it (2-3 sentences)
- **Goals**: 3-5 specific, measurable outcomes tied to user or business metrics
- **Non-Goals**: 3-5 things explicitly out of scope, with brief rationale for each
- **User Stories**: Standard format ("As a [user type], I want [capability] so that [benefit]"), grouped by persona
- **Requirements**: Categorized as Must-Hav

# Talk Coach — PRD v0.1

**Owner:** Francis (solo dev) · **Status:** Draft for MVP build · **Target ship:** 2–3 weekends to testable prototype

---

## 1. Problem Statement

People walk out of important conversations (first meetings, sales calls, dates, tough feedback) replaying what they said and wishing they'd said it differently — but by then the moment is gone. Existing tools (journals, coaches, therapists) give delayed, generic reflection. **No tool gives you feedback on *how you talked to this specific person* while the interaction is still fresh enough to act on.**

**Cost of not solving:** Users keep repeating the same conversational patterns (dominating, under-asserting, flat affect, missed cues) because the feedback loop between "what I said" and "how it landed" is too long — often never closed at all.

---

## 2. Target User

**Primary (V1):** Francis himself + 5–10 hand-picked testers who are:
- Professionals 25–40 doing high-stakes conversations weekly (sales, PM, founder, manager, dater).
- Already self-aware enough to want feedback, not defensive about it.
- iPhone owners, comfortable recording themselves.

**Explicitly not the target:** Casual users who want a "fun AI thing." This is a deliberate-practice tool for people trying to improve.

---

## 3. Jobs-to-be-Done

> **When** I just finished (or am in the middle of) a conversation that matters,
> **I want to** get specific, honest feedback on what I just said and how to say the next thing better,
> **so I can** adjust in real time or at least not repeat the same mistake tomorrow.

Secondary JTBD:
> When I'm preparing for a known hard conversation, I want to practice a line out loud and get a reaction, so I walk in less anxious.

---

## 4. Goals

| # | Goal | How we measure |
|---|------|----------------|
| G1 | Time from "stop recording" to feedback rendered **< 4 seconds** for a 30-second clip | Instrumented timer on device |
| G2 | Feedback is specific enough that tester can name a behavior to change after reading it | Weekly tester interview: "What did you change?" — count concrete answers |
| G3 | A tester records ≥3 conversations in their first week | SwiftData count in analytics ping |
| G4 | At least 1 tester says "I actually did X differently in a real conversation because of this" within 2 weeks | Qualitative — single quote is the bar |

**These are the only goals for V1.** Everything else is a distraction.

---

## 5. Non-Goals

| Non-Goal | Why not now |
|---|---|
| Real-time coaching during a live conversation | Latency + social awkwardness (phone on table mid-date) — post-hoc is 90% of the value at 10% of the complexity |
| Transcribing the *other person* (diarization) | SFSpeech doesn't diarize reliably; a one-sided transcript of the user's speech is enough for self-focused coaching |
| Meta Ray-Ban glasses as the audio source | DAT SDK 0.6.0 doesn't expose glasses audio streaming; designing for it now is speculative |
| A social / sharing layer | Privacy nightmare, not needed to prove the core loop |
| Scoring / gamification leaderboards | Turns coaching into performance theater; actively harmful to honest practice |
| Multi-language support | Ship English-only; validate the loop first |

---

## 6. User Flows

### Flow A — Post-hoc review (V1 core)
```
Open app
→ Coach tab
→ Pick context (First Meeting / Date / Sales / Conflict / Interview / Casual)
→ Tap Record
→ Speak (10s–3min)
→ Tap Stop
→ Within 4s see: Next-best-line · Keep doing · Adjust · One-line summary
→ (Auto-saved to History)
```

### Flow B — Rehearsal
```
Coach tab
→ Context picker includes "Rehearsal"
→ Type or speak the line the other person might say (optional scaffold)
→ Record your response
→ Get feedback
```
*(V1: just reuse Flow A with a Rehearsal context. No new UI.)*

### Flow C — History review
```
History tab
→ See list of prior reviews sorted by recency
→ Tap to see full transcript + feedback
→ Delete if embarrassing
```

---

## 7. Requirements

### P0 — Must have
